coredata.dc:title: Enhanced oxidase-mimic constructed by luminescent carbon dots loaded on MIL-53(Fe)-NO2 for dual-mode detection of gallic acid and biothiols in food and humans
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Junxue
Ma, Wenyan
Wang, Yufei
Gu, Qinfen
Pan, Qinhe
Zong, Siyu
Qin, Minghao
Li, Jiyang
coredata.dc:description: Monitoring of gallic acid (GA) in food and biothiols in humans is crucial for body health. Nanozyme-mediated colorimetric strategy for evaluating them has been widely applied nowadays, however, the inferior efficient and susceptible single-signal recognition limit its further application. Herein, a sensitive biosensor was first constructed for bimodal detection of GA and biothiols based on CDs@MIL-53(Fe)-NO2, prepared through a facile and time-saving microwave treatment. Benefiting from the excellent fluorescent and electron transfer properties of CDs, CDs@MIL-53(Fe)-NO2 exhibited significant enhanced blue fluorescence and oxidase-like activity, which could oxide colorless 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine without H2O2, and the blue product could quench the fluorescence of composite. The dual-mode assay based on such bifunctional nanozyme showed an extremely sensitivity towards GA/l-cysteine/homocysteine with the detection limit of 62/65/124 nM and 17/16/27 nM in colorimetric/fluorescent modes, respectively. The practicability in real samples and portability based on a smartphone of the analysis has been investigated with reliable results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Oxidase-mimic
Carbon dots
Metal-organic framework
Fluorescence
Colorimetry
Gallic acid

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Colorimetric-fluorescent dual-mode detection of GA, Cys and Hcy
Colorimetric and fluorescent detection of GA, Cys and Hcy

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Jilin Provincial Science and Technology Department
Key Research and Development Project of Hainan Province

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Jilin Province Science and Technology Development Plan
Specific Research Fund of the Innovation Platform for Academicians of Hainan Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We thank the Jilin Province Science and Technology Development Plan (20210402053GH), the 111 Project (No. B17020), and the Specific Research Fund of the Innovation Platform for Academicians of Hainan Province, China (YSPTZX202321) for financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of (A) MIL-53(Fe)-NO2 and (B) CM-2. (C) TEM image of CM-2. (D) XRD patterns and (E) FT-IR spectra of MIL-53(Fe)-NO2 and CDs@MIL-53(Fe)-NO2 composites. (F) XPS survey spectra, high-resolution XPS scan of (G) C 1s, (H) Fe 2p and (I) N 1s spectra of MIL-53(Fe)-NO2 (below) and CM-2 (above).

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of reaction solutions containing only TMB, CDs or pure MIL-53(Fe)-NO2 and TMB + different series of materials. (B) The effect of pH and time on the oxidase-like activity of CM-2. Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs under different excitations (C) and MIL-53(Fe)-NO2/CM-2 under the optimal excitation (D).

The kinetics curves of MIL-53(Fe)-NO2 (A) and CM-2 (C) with TMB as substrate, and the corresponding double reciprocal plots of MIL-53(Fe)-NO2 (B) and CM-2 (D). (E) EPR spectra and (F) the Nyquist curves of MIL-53(Fe)-NO2 and CM-2. (G) The overarching mechanistic process of CDs@MIL-53(Fe)-NO2 utilizing O2 to catalyze the oxidation of TMB.

Schematic diagrams of (A) colorimetric and (B) fluorescent sensor based on CM-2. (C) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CM-2 and UV–vis absorption spectrum of ox-TMB. (D) UV–vis absorption plots of CM-2 with TMB in the presence of different concentration of GA/Cys/Hcy. (E) and (F) The absorbance at 652 nm versus the GA and Cys concentration. Inset: the linear fit plot of ΔA and the increase of GA/Cys concentration. (G) Fluorescence plots of CM-2 with TMB in the presence of different concentration of GA/Cys/Hcy. (H) and (I) F/F0 versus the GA/Cys concentrations.

Selectivity of (A) colorimetric and (B) fluorescent detection of GA/Cys/Hcy with error bars corresponding to n = 3 (orange color: ions interferences, green color: amino acids interferences, and the purple color: biomolecules interferences). (C) Diagrammatic sketch of the detection GA/Cys of CM-2-based PAD. (D) The color of PAD with different amounts of GA/Cys. (E) and (F) Linear curves between (R + G)/2B and (G + B)/2R and GA dosage. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Illustration of preparation of CDs@MIL-53(Fe)-NO2 and application of composite based on the oxidase-like activity for dual-mode detection of GA and biothiols.

head.ce:title: Enhanced oxidase-mimic constructed by luminescent carbon dots loaded on MIL-53(Fe)-NO2 for dual-mode detection of gallic acid and biothiols in food and humans

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Liaoning Normal University, Huanghe Road 850#, Dalian 116029, China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Liaoning Normal University, Huanghe Road 850#, Dalian 116029, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Australian Synchrotron (ANSTO), 800 Blackburn Road, Clayton, VIC 3168, Australia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Advanced Materials of Tropical Island Resources, Ministry of Education, School of Chemical Engineering and Technology and College of Science, Hainan University, Haikou 570228, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Advanced Materials of Tropical Island Resources
School of Chemical Engineering and Technology and College of Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Advanced Materials of Tropical Island Resources, Ministry of Education, School of Chemical Engineering and Technology and College of Science, Hainan University, Haikou 570228, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) were introduced into MIL-53(Fe)-NO2 to construct an oxidase-mimic with high activity and strong fluorescence, and a sensitive colorimetric/fluorescent biosensor was first developed for detection of gallic acid/cysteine/homocysteine with an extremely low detection limit of 16/17/27 nM. The practicability in food and human samples and portability based on a smartphone of the analysis has been investigated with reliable results.

Monitoring of gallic acid (GA) in food and biothiols in humans is crucial for body health. Nanozyme-mediated colorimetric strategy for evaluating them has been widely applied nowadays, however, the inferior efficient and susceptible single-signal recognition limit its further application. Herein, a sensitive biosensor was first constructed for bimodal detection of GA and biothiols based on CDs@MIL-53(Fe)-NO2, prepared through a facile and time-saving microwave treatment. Benefiting from the excellent fluorescent and electron transfer properties of CDs, CDs@MIL-53(Fe)-NO2 exhibited significant enhanced blue fluorescence and oxidase-like activity, which could oxide colorless 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine without H2O2, and the blue product could quench the fluorescence of composite. The dual-mode assay based on such bifunctional nanozyme showed an extremely sensitivity towards GA/l-cysteine/homocysteine with the detection limit of 62/65/124 nM and 17/16/27 nM in colorimetric/fluorescent modes, respectively. The practicability in real samples and portability based on a smartphone of the analysis has been investigated with reliable results.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: It is crucial for human health to monitor health-related antioxidants ingested from food and biomolecules reflecting physical condition in the body. Antioxidants in human body can scavenge excessive free radicals to maintain the redox balance and effectively protect cellular organs from oxidative damage. However, the body can hardly manufacture antioxidants on its own and must obtain them from exogenous foods such as drinks and fruits, etc (Shang, Zhang, Ding, Zhang, & Wang, 2023). Gallic acid (GA), as a typical antioxidant, is frequently used in chemical, pharmaceutical, food, and drugs manufacturing. An affordable intake of GA in food is beneficial, but excessive consumption of GA can be harmful to one's health (Han et al., 2021; Lu, Zhang, & Huang, 2022). Biothiols, typical as glutathione (GSH), l-cysteine (Cys) and homocysteine (Hcy), are closely related to various biological process and various diseases will occur when they are at abnormal levels (Zhang, Song, Shao, & Li, 2021). Therefore, the quantitative analysis of GA in food and biothiols in humans, particularly achieving the simultaneous monitoring all of them are much required. Compared with the present methods for detecting GA and biothiols, such as electrochemical, HPLC, chemiluminescence, etc., optical assays based on artificial nanozyme biosensors have recently received growing attentions due to their unique advantages of cheapness, high activity, ease to operate, good practicality, and potential in rational design and functionalization (Li, Zhang, Wang, Lin, & Wei, 2022). Among various types of nanozymes, the peroxidase-like nanozymes are the most common for GA and biothiols detection, but H2O2 required is harmful, unstable, and decomposable. Considering the food safety and the accuracy of analysis outcomes, the developing of H2O2-free oxidase-mimics toward GA and biothiols are of great interest, because they can efficiently and selectively utilize O2 to catalyze the oxidation of substrates (Wu et al., 2019). In recent years, some typical oxidase mimics including carbon dots (Li et al., 2023), MnO2 nanosheets (Li et al., 2019), Co-N/C-900 (Chen et al., 2022), V/Co@NC (Lu et al., 2022), and Au@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) (Zhang et al., 2021), etc. have been developed for detection of GA and Cys. However, the overall detection limits (LOD) are at the level of dozens of nanomoles to few micromoles. To achieve the trace monitoring of GA in fine-grade foods and biothiols in the body, the innovation of high-performance oxidase-mimics is curial.

Furthermore, it is noted that the known nanozyme-mediated optical detection toward GA and biothiols all relies on single-mode method wherein the majority of that is colorimetry and very few is fluorometry, which is susceptible to interference and affects the accuracy of the results. Therefore, the development of dual/multi-mode biosensors for GA and biothiols detection is highly desired due to its inherent built-in cross-reference correction capability and the ability to corroborate output results with each other, both of which greatly improve accuracy and reliability (Liu et al., 2022; Du, Ke, Zhang, & Feng, 2022).

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) as a rising star among nanozymes, have drawn great interest because of their excellent stability and abundant active sites (Huang et al., 2021; Lai et al., 2022). Particularly, benefiting from the porosity of MOFs, such materials can be employed as a good matrix by loading other nanoparticles (e.g. nature enzymes, metal oxides) to improve their activity (Chen, Yang, Liu, & Li, 2019). Carbon dots (CDs) are a novel 0D carbon-based nanomaterials with low toxicity, good biocompatibility, and easy synthesis, which have been successfully employed for food freshness, bioimaging, oral cleaning, drug delivery, and tumor treatment. (Ma et al., 2022; Zong et al., 2022). Moreover, CDs have excellent luminescence and the ability to provide an electron transfer mediator, thus the introducing of CDs into MOFs not only can improve the catalytic performance of the composites, but also helps to construct a dual-mode detection platform (Chen, Liu, & Huang, 2020). For example, the integration of N-CDs and UIO-66 enabled the composite higher enzymatic activity than individual compositions and light regulating redox ability (Zhao et al., 2022). Another notable example is peroxidase-mimic of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr, which can realize the multimodal (fluorescence, temperature and colorimetric) detection of glyphosate (Luo et al., 2023). However, by now, there is still no oxidase-mimics of CDs@MOF nanozymes without additional external stimulation. In addition, it is still a huge challenge for development of dual/multi-mode optical platform for detecting GA and biothiols based on nanozymes.

On the basis of above considerations, we successfully constructed an efficient H2O2-free nanozyme biosensor to firstly achieve the dual-mode detection of GA and biothiols (e.g. Cys/Hcy) by rational pre-design and selection of components. Such oxidase-mimic was composed of porous MIL-53(Fe)-NO2 with broad 1D channels and luminescent N,O-rich CDs with narrower band gap, which was prepared by a facile and time-saving microwave treatment replacing traditional solvothermal method. The introduction of CDs endowed the composite higher oxidase-like activity than MIL-53(Fe)-NO2 (Km: 0.073 mM vs 0.162 mM), as well as enhanced blue fluorescence (8-fold higher than MIL-53(Fe)-NO2). As a result, CDs@MIL-53(Fe)-NO2 could effectively oxide TMB into a blue ox-TMB without external stimulation, and ox-TMB was reduced to initial TMB by GA/Cys/Hcy, during which the fluorescence of composite was first quenched and then recovered (Scheme 1 ). Owing to the high activity and changes of dual signals, a sensitive bimodal platform was developed to detect GA/Cys/Hcy and an extremely low LOD of 62/65/124 nM and 17/16/27 nM in colorimetric/fluorescent mode was achieved, respectively. Furthermore, the detection in food and human samples, and an easy paper-based analytical devices for naked-eye analysis were investigated and developed, as well as obtained with satisfied results, revealing the good accuracy and practicality of the assay.

Citric acid (CA) and ethylenediamine (EDA) were purchased from Tianjin GuangFu Technoligy Development Co.,LTD (Tianjin, China) and Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China), respectively. N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF), anhydrous ethanol, acetic acid (HAc) were obtained from Tianjin jingdongtianzheng Precision Chemical Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China). Sodium acetate (NaAc), ZnCl2, AlCl3, NiCl2, NaCl and KCl were purchased from Beijing Chemical Works Co. Ltd. (Beijing, China). Iron chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O) and ascorbic acid (AA) were purchased from Tianjin FuChen Reagent Co.,Ltd (Tianjin, China). Nitroterephthalic acid (BDC-NO2), TMB, salicylic acid (SA), dopamine (DA), l-cysteine (Cys), homocysteine (Hcy) and glutathione (GSH) were provided by Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Threonine (Thr), serine (Ser), histidine (His), isoleucine (Ile), glutamine (Glu), proline (Pro), valine (Val), methionine (Met), alanine (Ala), aspartic acid (Asp), glucose and uric acid (UA) were bought from Alfa (Shanghai, China). All the chemicals were obtained from commercial sources and used as received.

UV–vis adsorption spectra were recorded on Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrophotometer. The infrared (IR) absorption spectrum was taken on a Bruker FT-IR IFS-66 V/S spectrometer. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was performed on a JEOL JSM-6700F SEM. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images were obtained on an FEI Tecnai G2 S-Twin F20 transmission electron microscope. Photoluminescence spectra were carried out by a HORIBA Scientific Fluoromax-4 spectrofluorometer. Power X-ray diffraction (XRD) data were conducted by Rigaku D/max-2550 diffractometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were obtained on a Thermo ESCALAB250 spectrometer with monochromatized Al Kα excitation. The Zeta potential was measured by using Malvern Zetaszier Nano-ZS. The electrochemical analysis was performed on a CHI 660D electrochemical workstation (Shanghai, China). The EPR spectra were obtained on a JES-FA 200 EPR spectrometer.

Briefly, 4.8 g CA and 1.675 mL EDA were dissolved in 50 mL deionized water to form homogeneous solution, which was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated for 6 h at 200 °C (Pei et al., 2022). The dark suspension was obtained after cooled down to room temperature and then spin-drying to obtain CDs solid powder.

CDs@MIL-53(Fe)-NO2 was prepared by a time-saving microwave treatment. Briefly, 1.350 g FeCl3·6H2O and 0.524 g BDC-NO2 were dissolved in 15 mL DMF, then 1 mL HAc was added into the solution and stirred for 20 min. Thereafter, different amounts of CDs powder (4.6, 10.8, 13, and 16 mg) was added to MIL-53(Fe)-NO2 precursor solution. Each mixture was sonicated for 15 min and transferred to the Teflon tub before heating at 170 °C for 30 min in a microwave. After cooling down to room temperature, the suspension was centrifuged under 8000 rpm for 4 min, the obtained solid was washed three times with DMF and ethanol, and dried in vacuum. The final products were named CM-1, CM-2, CM-3, and CM-4, respectively. The control product (pure MIL-53(Fe)-NO2) was prepared by the same process only without CDs.

The oxidase-like activity of materials was evaluated by using TMB as the chromogenic substrate. 40 µL materials (4 mg/mL), 40 μL TMB (10 mM) were added into 1940 µL acetate buffer solution (0.2 M, pH 3.0). Then UV–vis absorption of the mixed solution was recorded. The steady-state kinetic analysis was calculated according to Michaelis-Menten Eq. (1)
(1)
1 V
=
K m
V max
1 [S]
+
1
V max

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric-fluorescent dual-mode detection of GA, Cys and Hcy
ce:section.ce:para: For GA/Cys/Hcy detection, different concentrations of analytes were added into the system containing 40 µL CM-2 (4 mg/mL), 120 µL TMB (10 mM) and buffer (0.2 M, pH 3.0). Then, the mixture was incubated at 45 °C for 25 min, the degree of oxidation of TMB and fluorescence intensity was monitored by UV–vis and fluorescence spectra, respectively, and the corresponding graphs were made.

To investigate the feasibility of our method in real samples, green tea drink samples purchased from the school supermarket and human serum samples obtained from local hospital were chosen for recovery experiments. The human serum samples were centrifuged at 10500 rpm for 15 min. Then different concentrations of analytes standard solution were added to the green tea and serum samples, respectively. The spiked samples were determined as described in section 2.6.

For naked-eye analysis, firstly, 20 μL CM-2 (500 μg/mL) was uniformly spread over the paper surface. Then the paper was dried at room temperature, 20 μL GA/Cys solution with a series of concentrations was dripped into the paper. After incubating for 8 min, the buffer (pH 3.0) with TMB (1 mM) was also dropped and stand for another 8 min. Finally, the images of the test paper were achieved and the red, green, and blue (RGB) values were measured by a Color Recognition APP in a smartphone, and the corresponding analysis of GA/Cys was achieved.

The morphology and size of as-synthesized materials were shown in Fig. 1 A and B. After loading CDs, the composites presented the unchanged long spindle-like morphology as MIL-53(Fe)-NO2, but a shorter length and a narrower width of size, e.g. CM-2 and MIL-53(Fe)-NO2 crystal size is ca. 4 µm × 1 µm and 11 µm × 2 µm, respectively. The TEM image in Fig. S1A showed that the prepared CDs had good dispersion and uniform size about 2 nm. A broad diffraction peak is observed in XRD pattern of CDs, which is attributed to the (002) planes of graphite lattice spacing (Fig. S1C). As for the composite represented by CM-2, some dark spherical particles (Fig. 1C, S1B) could be seen clearly, indicating that CDs had been loaded successfully on MIL-53(Fe)-NO2. The XRD patterns showed the crystallographic structures of MIL-53(Fe)-NO2 and all composites (Fig. 1D), indicating the successful preparation of MIL-53(Fe)-NO2 (Wu et al., 2021; Cheng, Zhou, Zheng, & Kuang, 2022). Regretfully, the lattice spacing of CDs on CM-2 could not be obtained due to the small size of CDs and shading of matrix MIL-53(Fe)-NO2.

The FT-IR spectra ascertained the functional groups of materials (Fig. 1E). As we expected, the prepared CDs contained the nitrogen–oxygen functional groups (Ma, Wang, Yang, & Li, 2021; Liu et al., 2022). The composites had similar peaks at 1662, 1547, 1388, 775, and 547 cm−1, but their CO vibration peaks shifted from 1699 to 1662 cm−1 compared to CDs, which might be explained by the electrical interactions between MIL-53(Fe)-NO2 and CDs (He, Liang, Zhou, Yan, & Wu, 2021). The peak at 1547 cm−1 was due to the NO stretching vibration of the ligands of MIL-53(Fe)-NO2 (Zhu, He, & Wang, 2020). The CH stretching vibrations of benzene was located at 775 cm−1 (Luo et al., 2022), and the peak at 547 cm−1 was due to the Fe-O stretching mode, indicating the Fe-oxo bonds are formed between the ligand and Fe3+ (Nguyen et al., 2021).

The elemental composition and chemical state of materials were identified by the XPS analysis. Fig. S2 A demonstrated the elements of C, O and N in CDs. More detailed information was received by higher-resolution XPS spectra (Fig. S2B-D), which further proved CDs composed of rich nitrogen–oxygen functional groups. For MIL-53(Fe)-NO2 and CM-2, Fig. 1F displayed the existence of C, N, O, and Fe. The C 1 s was fitted to the CC, CO/CN and CO (Fig. 1G) (He et al., 2021). The N 1 s exhibited the presence of NO and NC species (Fig. 1I) (Zhu et al., 2020). The fitted peaks had no much differences due to the presence of ligands in MIL-53(Fe)-NO2. Nevertheless, the peaks of CO, CO/CN and NO of CM-2 exhibited larger peak area than those of MIL-53(Fe)-NO2, which should be caused by loading CDs. Furthermore, the CHN elemental analysis also proved that composites had higher contents of organic species than MIL-53(Fe)-NO2 (Table S1). In addition, the O 1 s spectra were ascribed to COFe and Fe-O-Fe (Fig. S3) (He et al., 2021). The Fe 2p spectra displayed the peaks of Fe 2p3/2, Fe 2p1/2 and satellite, indicating the main Fe3+ species contained in the material (Fig. 1H) (Qiang, Chen, & Qin, 2021). Notably, the Fe 2p3/2 peak moved to a higher binding energy in CM-2, causing the electron deficiency of Fe3+ and making them better electron acceptors. This result implied the alteration of chemical environment of Fe3+ in CM-2 caused by interactions between CDs and MIL-53(Fe)-NO2 (He et al., 2021).

After loading CDs, the composites presented enhanced oxidase-like activity and fluorescence. The oxidase-mimetic activity was investigated by employing TMB as substrate. When only existing TMB, CDs or MIL-53(Fe)-NO2 in the reaction solutions, there was no absorption (Fig. 2A), excluding the interference of themselves. There was also no absorption when CDs mixed with TMB, indicating CDs could not oxidize TMB without external stimulus. Interestingly, a strong absorption at 652 nm appeared when the reaction solutions containing both TMB and MIL-53(Fe)-NO2 or TMB and CDs@MIL-53(Fe)-NO2 composites, suggesting TMB was oxidized by these materials. It was worth noting that the oxidase-like activity of MIL-53(Fe)-NO2 was enhanced after loading CDs, and CM-2 composite displayed the best catalytic performance. Thus, we employed CM-2 as a representative for the follow-up research. As a common characteristic of enzymes, their catalytic ability is greatly affected by the reaction environment (Yang et al., 2021). Therefore, the pH, temperature and reaction time were optimized. From Fig. 2B and S4A, pH 3.0, 45 °C and 25 min were determined to be the optimal oxidase-mimicking condition for CM-2 composite.

Subsequently, the fluorescence emission spectra were conducted to study the luminescent properties of CDs, MIL-53(Fe)-NO2 and CM-2 composite. Under the optimal oxidase-mimicking pH condition, it was observed that CDs exhibited the excitation-dependent feature, and the optimal emission and excitation wavelengths were 370 and 454 nm, respectively (Fig. 2C). MIL-53(Fe)-NO2 showed a weak blue fluorescence at 455 nm under optimal excitation at 360 nm originated from the organic ligands (Zhu et al., 2020). Strikingly, under 370 nm excitation, CDs@MIL-53(Fe)-NO2 showed strong blue fluorescence at 453 nm, the intensity of fluorescence was 8-fold higher than that of pure MIL-53(Fe)-NO2 (Fig. 2D). This was attributed to the introduction of excellent luminescent CDs.

The steady-state kinetic experiments were performed by using different TMB concentrations to further investigate the degree of increase in oxidase-like activity before (Fig. 3 A, B) and after (Fig. C, D) the incorporation of CDs. The absorbance at 652 nm was recorded and Km was calculated. The Km value of CM-2 (0.073 mM) was much smaller than that of MIL-53(Fe)-NO2 (0.162 mM), indicating a higher affinity for TMB in CM-2 and pure MIL-53(Fe)-NO2 was inferior to CM-2 composite in terms of oxidase-like activity. Compared with the various MOFs-related oxidase-like mimics (Table S2), CM-2 realized the first oxidase-mimetic of CDs@MOFs without additional stimulation, its Km value was lower than most of MOF-based nanozymes and just higher than that of Mn-Co ZIF (Zhang et al., 2019), mixed-valence-state cobalt (Wang, Su, Lin, Yang, & Yang, 2018) and NCDs/UiO-66 (with stimulation) (Zhao et al., 2022). Importantly, CM-2 composite exhibited excellent stability under room temperature. The relative enzyme-like activity was only decreased 2% after 15 days, reflecting the good storage stability of CM-2 (Fig. S4 B). These results suggest that CM-2 composite has good potential application prospect for acting as a superior oxidase mimic.

The catalytic mechanism was further explored. It can be seen the catalytic activity of CM-2 was suppressed when O2 lacking (Fig. S5 A). According to previous studies, nanozymes normally transformed O2 into active oxygen species and employed them to catalyze the reaction (Yang et al., 2021). Thus, the type of active oxygen species was further determined by scavenging experiments. Only the p-benzoquinone groups' signal showed significant differences versus that of the control group, showing O2 •− was the active oxygen species in the catalytic process (Fig. S5B). Furthermore, O2 •− was identified by EPR with DMPO as the spin probe (Luo et al., 2022) and the distinctive signals related with O2 •− could be observed with the addition of MIL-53(Fe)-NO2 and CM-2 (Fig. 3E). The stronger signal intensity of CM-2 demonstrated that CDs did provide MIL-53(Fe)-NO2 with greater oxidase-like activity.

The electrochemical investigation was completed to explore the process in more detail. In Fig. S6A, CDs showed two peaks assigned to the π-π* transition of CC and the n-π* transition of CO (Liu et al., 2022). The highest absorption band in MIL-53(Fe)-NO2 and CM-2 was observed at about 350 nm owing to ligand O(II)-to-metal Fe(III) charge transfer (Nguyen et al., 2021). The relevant Tauc plots was acquired based on the eq2 (Qiang et al., 2021).
(2) αhv = A(hv-Eg)n/2

In view of the above results, CM-2 shows more superior oxidase-like activity due to the following reasons: (i) the smaller particle size of CM-2 makes it larger contact area with the substrate; (ii) the more negative zeta potential value of CM-2 than that of pure MIL-53(Fe)-NO2 facilitates to bind with the positively charged TMB substrate (Fig. S7); (iii) the incorporation of CDs promotes the electron transfer between substrate, oxygen, and catalysts. The overarching mechanistic process is illustrated in Fig. 3G. The CM-2 surface first absorbs oxygen, which is then triggered to create O2 •−. The action of the electrons from O2 •− to the reaction substrate TMB is promoted by the excellent electron transport capabilities of CDs, resulting in the rapid oxidation of TMB by O2 •−. For the pure MIL-53(Fe)-NO2, the catalytic performance is at a comparatively low level due to the absence of electron transport medium of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric and fluorescent detection of GA, Cys and Hcy
ce:section.ce:para: Attributed to the anti-oxidation property of GA and highly reductive property of biothiols, all of them could transform the ox-TMB to TMB (Fig. 4A). Considering larger steric hindrance of GSH that will influence the reactivity of –SH groups and their interactions with materials, low weights biothiols, such as Cys and Hcy, have been utilized as the analytes. The results showed that the UV–vis absorption decreased with the increase of GA, Cys or Hcy concentrations and a simple colorimetric assay was established to detect their color changes (Fig. 4D). The changing of absorbance (ΔA = A-A0, where A and A0 represented the absorbance at 652 nm before and after GA/Cys/Hcy addition) showed a good linearity to GA/Cys/Hcy concentration in the range of 0–12/0–15/0–12 μM, and the LOD (LOD = 3σ/k, where σ is the standard deviation for the blank solution at an S/N ratio of 10, which is 0.15%, and k is the slope of ΔA652 versus the GA/Cys/Hcy concentration) at an S/N ratio of 3 for GA, Cys and Hcy is 0.062, 0.065 and 0.124 μM, respectively (Fig. 4E, F and Fig. S8A). As shown in Table S3, 4 and 5, most of previous nanozyme biosensors for GA, Cys and Hcy detection are based on peroxidase-mimics, H2O2-free oxidase-mimics are very few. CM-2 presented in this work possessed excellent oxidase-like activity, and exhibited superior sensitivity in colorimetry towards GA, Cys and Hcy.

Moreover, ox-TMB could quench the fluorescence of MIL-53(Fe)-NO2 and CM-2. However, the quench degree of ox-TMB on CM-2 was more significant because CM-2 had stronger fluorescence and higher catalytic ability generating more ox-TMB (Fig. S8C). The quenching mechanism was explored. It can be seen a considerable overlap existed between the excitation spectrum of CM-2 and the absorption spectrum of ox-TMB (Fig. 4C). The fluorescence lifetime of CM-2 barely changed after the reaction (Fig. S9A) and there was no new UV–vis absorption peak appeared, indicating without new complex formed in the quenching process (Fig. S9B), which excluded the possibility of static quenching. These results verified that the quenching phenomenon was caused by the inner filtering effect (IFE). Based on the quenching interaction shown in Fig. 4B, a sensitive fluorescent assay was developed to detect GA, Cys and Hcy (Fig. 4G). The intensity of F/F0 (where F and F0 represented the fluorescence intensity at 453 nm before and after GA/Cys/Hcy addition) displayed a good linearity versus GA/Cys/Hcy concentrations in the range of 0–20/0–12/0–25 μM with an extremely low LOD (LOD = 3σ/k, where σ is the standard deviation for the blank solution at an S/N ratio of 10, which is 0.1%, and k is the slope of F/F0 versus the GA/Cys/Hcy concentration) of 17/16/27 nM, respectively (Fig. 4H, I and Fig. S8B). It is noted that there are rare reports for nanozyme-based fluorescent detection of GA/Cys/Hcy (Table S3, S4 and S5). Compared with previous nanozymes, CM-2 not only enriched the types of efficient oxidase-mimics, but also achieved the first nanozyme-mediated dual-mode (colorimetric and fluorescent) detection for GA/Cys/Hcy. As far as we known, CM-2 exhibited comparable to the best sensitivity towards GA/Cys/Hcy in all related nanozyme-mediated sensing platforms.

Besides, the selectivity analysis was studied by adding other interfering substances into the sensing system, including K+, Na+, Zn2+, Al3+, Ni2+, Thr, Ser, His, Iso, Glu, Pro, Val, Met, Ala, Asp, glucose, UA, SA, DA, CA, AA, and GSH. Our method showed an excellent selectivity because only the signal of GA, Cys and Hcy had changed significantly (Fig. 5A, B). Thereafter, standard addition experiments were carried out to investigate the practicability of this method in food and human samples (green tea drink and human serum), and then the concentration of the target analytes in real sample were calculated according to the working curve given above. The assay displayed the good recovery of 98.91–100.07% for GA, 99.76–101.4% for Cys and 99.74–100.3% for Hcy, and the low RSD less than 4.5%, revealing a good accuracy of this test for GA, Cys and Hcy detection (Table S6). These results indicate that CM-2 nanozyme is a promising candidate in food and biological analytical field.

In order to enhance the practicality and convenience of such nanozyme and broaden the scope of application, we strived to develop an easy paper-based analytical devices (PAD) for naked-eye analysis. CM-2-based PAD was first fabricated, GA and Cys was selected as representatives for the next analysis (Fig. 5C). As seen in Fig. 5D, when only CM-2 existing on the surface of test paper, the PAD displayed an obvious blue color due to the oxidation effect of CM-2 on TMB. Nevertheless, the color faded with the addition of GA/Cys due to its anti-oxidation and reductive property. For quantitative analysis of GA/Cys, the color and RGB values of PAD were acquired by a color Application in a smartphone. As shown in Fig. 5E-F, Fig. S10A-B and Fig. S11, the (R + G)/2B, (G + B)/2R, (R + B)/2G and R/B ratio showed a good linearity with GA/Cys concentration to confirm the feasibility of this PAD detection and the LOD (LOD = 3σ/k, where σ is the standard deviation for the blank solution at an S/N ratio of 10, which is 0.2%, and k is the slope of (R + G)/2B, (G + B)/2R, (R + B)/2G or R/B versus the GA/Cys concentration) was calculated to be 1.04/1.24 μM for (R + G)/2B and 0.87/0.91 μM for (G + B)/2R model in the range of 2–30/2–20 μM, respectively. These results indicate that CM-2 nanozyme has the potential to conduct an accurate and portable analysis for food safety.

In summary, a nanozyme-mediated bimodal colorimetric/fluorescent sensing platform for GA and biothiols detection had been first constructed by utilizing oxidase-mimic of CDs@MIL-53(Fe)-NO2 with high activity, prepared by a facile and time-saving microwave treatment through doping different content of CDs into MIL-53(Fe)-NO2. The host–guest synergy effect made CDs@MIL-53(Fe)-NO2 higher activity and stronger blue fluorescence. The sensing platform was operated owing to the influences of analytes on both of oxidase-like activity and fluorescence of CM-2 + TMB system, which showed highly sensitivity and selectivity for GA/Cys/Hcy detection with extremely low LOD of 17/16/27 nM, respectively. The stability, reliable analysis in food and human samples, and naked-eye analysis based on PAD had been investigated as well, proving its great potential application in biological and food analytical field. This work solved the problem that the existing CDs@MOFs nanozymes have no oxidase-like activity in the absence of external stimulation, and would offer more possibilities to develop high-efficiency hybrid nanozymes as dual/multi-mode biosensors by designing and screening adapted CDs and MOFs to provide higher security for food safety.

Junxue Liu: Conceptualization, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Wenyan Ma: Formal analysis, Data curation. Yufei Wang: Formal analysis. Qinfen Gu: Investigation. Qinhe Pan: Investigation. Siyu Zong: Resources. Minghao Qin: Resources. Jiyang Li: Supervision, Validation, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank the Jilin Province Science and Technology Development Plan (20210402053GH), the 111 Project (No. B17020), and the Specific Research Fund of the Innovation Platform for Academicians of Hainan Province, China (YSPTZX202321) for financial support.
